query: do ACE inhibitors and ARBs increase the risk of severe COVID-19?

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Manifestations of Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Early in 2020, a controversy grew regarding the safe usage of ACE inhibitors (ACEI) and angiotensin receptor-blocking (ARB) drugs in patients infected with COVID-19. The current consensus agrees to the continued use of these medications. [27] [28] The BRACE CORONA trial found no significant difference in the number of days alive and out of hospital through 30 days among subjects receiving continuous ACEI/ARB and hospitalized for COVID-19 compared to those in whom these medicines were temporarily suspended. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: ACE Inhibitors -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Post-marketing surveillance: Significant adverse events are reported in less than one percent of the population in post-marketing surveillance. These adverse drug reactions include anaphylactoid reactions, cardiac arrest, cutaneous pseudolymphoma, eosinophilic pneumonitis, hepatic necrosis, hyponatremia, intestinal angioedema, malignant neoplasm of the lung, pulmonary embolism, systemic lupus erythematosus, Stevens-Johnson Syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis, and transient ischemic attack. Khera R. et al. found no association between ACE inhibitors and angiotensin II receptor blockers in COVID-19 hospitalization and mortality. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Manifestations of Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: According to the latest United States National Institutes of Health guidelines, ACEI and ARB therapy should be continued for their underlying cardiac indication unless the acute status warrants withholding the medication (such as acute renal failure). The American Heart Association, the Heart Failure Society of America, and the American College of Cardiology issued a joint statement stating that renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system antagonists should not be added or removed for a diagnosis of COVID-19 infection. [28]

retrieved_docs.content: The RBD is a fundamental peptide in the pathogenesis of infection as it represents a binding site for the human angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptors. Inhibition of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) does not increase the risk of hospitalization for COVID-19 and severe disease. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Angiotensin II Receptor Blockers (ARB) -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: A lower dose of ARBs should be considered in adults aged 65 years or older due to increased sensitivity to medication and the potential for adverse effects. This adjustment helps minimize the risk of hypotension and other related complications in this population.

retrieved_docs.title: ACE Inhibitors -- Contraindications -- Relative Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: First-Dose Hypotension: Profound first-dose hypotension may occur when ACE inhibitors are introduced to patients with heart failure who are already taking a high dose of loop diuretics.

retrieved_docs.title: ACE Inhibitors -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of ACE inhibitors. Evaluate the adverse drug reactions of ACE inhibitors. Determine the classic indications for initiating ACE inhibitor therapy. Implement an interprofessional team approach that utilizes evidence-based medicine and a patient-centered approach to enhance patient outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme Inhibitors (ACEI) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: ACE inhibitors are medications used to treat and manage hypertension, which is a significant risk factor for coronary disease, heart failure, stroke, and a host of other cardiovascular conditions. Most cases are primary and not attributable to any specific etiology. This activity reviews the indications, contraindications, activity, adverse events, and other key elements of ACE inhibitor therapy in the clinical setting related to the essential points needed by members of an interprofessional team managing the care of patients with hypertension and its related conditions and sequelae.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1957
retrieved_docs.content: ACE INHIBITORS, PLAIN is a pharmacologic class of angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors that includes the agents delapril, imidapril, lisinopril, perindopril, spirapril, trandolapril, and zofenopril. All of these drugs share the same core mechanism of inhibiting the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, which results in vasodilation, reduced aldosterone secretion, and decreased sympathetic activity. Consequently, each member of the ACE INHIBITORS, PLAIN class is used to manage conditions such as hypertension, heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy, and they collectively carry class‑related considerations including the risk of cough, hyperkalemia, and potential impacts on renal function. Recognizing that delapril, imidapril, lisinopril, perindopril, spirapril, trandolapril, and zofenopril are all members of this ACE inhibitor group aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy and monitoring for shared adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2602
retrieved_docs.content: Benazepril, cilazapril, fosinopril, imidapril, lisinopril, perindopril and quinapril are all classified as plain angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors. As single‑agent members of the ACE‑inhibitor drug class, each of these agents works by blocking the conversion of angiotensin I to the potent vasoconstrictor angiotensin II, thereby reducing systemic vascular resistance, lowering blood pressure, and decreasing aldosterone‑mediated sodium and water retention. Because they are “plain” formulations, they are administered without additional antihypertensive components, allowing clinicians to tailor therapy and combine them with other drug classes as needed for optimal management of hypertension, heart failure, diabetic nephropathy and other conditions driven by an overactive renin‑angiotensin system. Recognizing that benazepril, cilazapril, fosinopril, imidapril, lisinopril, perindopril and quinapril share this common mechanism and classification helps guide prescribing decisions, monitor for class‑related adverse effects such as cough or hyperkalemia, and supports evidence‑based patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 981
retrieved_docs.content: ACE inhibitors (plain) constitute a pharmacologic class that includes the individual agents benazepril, delapril, enalapril, fosinopril, imidapril, lisinopril, moexipril, perindopril, quinapril, ramipril, spirapril, trandolapril and zofenopril. All of these drugs share the core mechanism of inhibiting the angiotensin‑converting enzyme, thereby reducing the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, which leads to vasodilation, decreased aldosterone secretion, and lowered blood pressure. As plain ACE inhibitors, they are commonly employed in the management of hypertension, heart failure, diabetic nephropathy, and for renal protection. Recognizing these agents as members of the same class guides clinicians in selecting appropriate monotherapy, monitoring for class‑related adverse effects such as cough and angioedema, and anticipating drug‑drug interaction potential.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1932
retrieved_docs.content: Ace inhibitors, plain constitute a pharmacologic class of angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors, and the agents benazepril, perindopril, quinapril, ramipril, spirapril and zofenopril are all members of this class. Each of these plain ACE inhibitors shares the same mechanism of action—blocking the conversion of angiotensin I to the vasoconstrictor angiotensin II—thereby reducing systemic vascular resistance, lowering blood pressure, and providing therapeutic benefit in conditions such as hypertension, heart failure, and post‑myocardial infarction remodeling. Because they belong to the same drug class, they have similar efficacy profiles, common adverse effects (including cough, hyperkalemia and renal function changes), and comparable drug‑interaction considerations, which is important for clinicians when selecting or substituting one plain ACE inhibitor for another in patient care.

Recognizing delapril, quinapril, ramipril, spirapril, trandolapril, and zofenopril as members of the ACE INHIBITORS, PLAIN class aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy and anticipating shared pharmacologic properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2521
retrieved_docs.content: Ace inhibitors, plain constitute a pharmacologic class of angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors, and the class includes the individual agents fosinopril, lisinopril, moexipril, spirapril, and trandolapril. All five medications share the same mechanism of action—blocking the conversion of angiotensin I to the potent vasoconstrictor angiotensin II—resulting in vasodilation, reduced aldosterone secretion, and decreased blood pressure. Because they belong to the same drug class, these agents are used interchangeably in the management of hypertension, heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy, and they exhibit similar class‑related adverse effects such as dry cough, hyperkalaemia, and the risk of angioedema. Recognizing that fosinopril, lisinopril, moexipril, spirapril, and trandolapril are members of the ace inhibitors, plain group aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy, monitoring for expected side effects, and understanding the shared therapeutic benefits across these medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1985
retrieved_docs.content: Benazepril, cilazapril, moexipril, perindopril, quinapril, ramipril, and zofenopril are all members of the drug class referred to as “ACE inhibitors, plain.” This class of angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors is used in clinical practice to lower blood pressure, treat heart failure, and provide renal protection in certain patient populations. By sharing the same mechanism of inhibiting the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, these agents collectively contribute to vasodilation, reduced aldosterone secretion, and decreased sympathetic activity, making them integral components of cardiovascular and renal disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1958
retrieved_docs.content: Ace inhibitors, plain constitute a pharmacologic class of angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors that are administered without combination with other antihypertensive agents. This class includes benazepril, cilazapril, fosinopril, moexipril, quinapril and ramipril, each of which shares the core mechanism of blocking the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, thereby reducing vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion and sympathetic activation. By lowering systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure, these agents are commonly used to treat hypertension, chronic heart failure, and to provide renal protection in patients with diabetes or proteinuric kidney disease. Because they act on the same enzymatic pathway, the plain ACE inhibitors have similar therapeutic benefits as well as comparable adverse effect profiles, including a dry cough, hyperkalemia, hypotension and the rare risk of angioedema. Understanding that benazepril, cilazapril, fosinopril, moexipril, quinapril and ramipril are all members of the ace inhibitors, plain group helps clinicians select appropriate monotherapy, anticipate class‑related side effects, and consider drug‑specific pharmacokinetic properties when individualizing patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2518
retrieved_docs.content: Ace inhibitors, plain constitute a therapeutic class of angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors that are administered without additional drug combinations, and benazepril, fosinopril, moexipril, perindopril, trandolapril, and zofenopril are all members of this class. Each of these agents shares the core mechanism of blocking the conversion of angiotensin I to the vasoconstrictor angiotensin II, thereby lowering systemic blood pressure, reducing afterload, and providing renal and cardiovascular protection. Although they belong to the same pharmacologic group, benazepril, fosinopril, moexipril, perindopril, trandolapril, and zofenopril differ in pharmacokinetic characteristics such as half‑life, dosing frequency, and metabolic pathways, which allows clinicians to tailor ACE‑inhibitor therapy to individual patient needs, tolerability, and comorbid conditions. Recognizing that these six drugs are plain ACE inhibitors helps guide appropriate selection, monitoring, and management of patients requiring renin‑angiotensin‑aldosterone system blockade.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1430
retrieved_docs.content: Benazepril, fosinopril, lisinopril, moexipril, perindopril, and spirapril are all plain ACE inhibitors, meaning they are single‑agent formulations of angiotensin‑converting enzyme inhibitors. As members of the ACE inhibitor class, they block the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, producing vasodilation, reduced aldosterone secretion, and lower blood pressure. These agents are commonly used to treat hypertension, heart failure, post‑myocardial infarction remodeling, and diabetic nephropathy. Because they share the same mechanism of action, they have comparable efficacy and safety profiles, although individual drugs may differ in dosing frequency, pharmacokinetics, and tolerability of side effects such as cough. Choice among them is guided by patient‑specific factors, including renal function, risk of adverse effects, and convenience of once‑daily dosing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1699
retrieved_docs.content: Cilazapril, imidapril, lisinopril, moexipril, perindopril, and zofenopril are all classified as plain angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors. Each of these agents belongs to the ACE inhibitor drug class, sharing the common mechanism of blocking the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, which results in vasodilation, reduced aldosterone secretion, and decreased blood pressure. Because of these pharmacologic effects, the plain ACE inhibitors are widely used in the management of hypertension, heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy, and they contribute similarly to cardiovascular risk reduction and organ protection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2224
retrieved_docs.content: Delapril, fosinopril, imidapril, lisinopril, moexipril, spirapril, and zofenopril are all classified as plain angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors. Each of these agents belongs to the broader drug class “ACE INHIBITORS, PLAIN,” indicating that they share the common mechanism of inhibiting ACE to reduce the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, thereby lowering blood pressure and providing cardiovascular protection. Recognizing that all seven medications are members of this ACE inhibitor class is important for clinicians when selecting appropriate antihypertensive therapy, considering drug‑specific pharmacokinetics, potential side‑effects, and patient‑specific factors.

These agents share the common mechanism of inhibiting the angiotensin‑converting enzyme and are used in the management of conditions such as hypertension and heart failure, allowing clinicians to consider class‑related effects and therapeutic options across these specific drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1918
retrieved_docs.content: ACE INHIBITORS, PLAIN is a pharmacologic class that encompasses several individual ACE‑inhibiting agents, including cilazapril, delapril, enalapril, fosinopril, imidapril, lisinopril, and moexipril. Each of these drugs shares the core mechanism of blocking the angiotensin‑converting enzyme, thereby reducing the formation of angiotensin II and leading to vasodilation, decreased aldosterone secretion, and lowered blood pressure. Because they belong to the same ACE INHIBITORS, PLAIN group, they are commonly used in the management of hypertension, heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy, and they exhibit similar therapeutic goals and side‑effect profiles, such as cough, hyperkalemia, and renal function monitoring. Recognizing that cilazapril, delapril, enalapril, fosinopril, imidapril, lisinopril, and moexipril are all members of the ACE INHIBITORS, PLAIN class helps clinicians select appropriate agents within this category while considering individual pharmacokinetic properties and patient‑specific factors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2153
retrieved_docs.content: Plain angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors comprise a specific group of antihypertensive agents that share a common mechanism of blocking the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II. The medications benazepril, cilazapril, fosinopril, lisinopril, trandolapril, and zofenopril are all members of this plain ACE‑inhibitor class. As members of the same pharmacologic class, these drugs are used to lower blood pressure, reduce afterload in heart failure, and provide renal protection in diabetic nephropathy, among other cardiovascular benefits. Recognizing that these six agents belong to the same therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate ACE‑inhibitor therapy, consider class‑wide side effects such as cough or angioedema, and apply consistent dosing and monitoring strategies across the group.
